summary: Jeff Atwood calculates the real cost of running PCs 24/7, discovering his server costs $500/year and his media center PC $205/year in electricity. He walks through the math of converting watts to kilowatt-hours and applying local electricity rates to determine annual costs. The post then provides practical tips for reducing power consumption on always-on machines, from configuring sleep settings to choosing efficient power supplies and avoiding power-hungry video cards. Atwood emphasizes that understanding actual power draw through measurement is essential before making optimization decisions.
key_points: A 160-watt server running 24/7 costs roughly $500/year at California electricity rates (36 cents/kWh)
The Kill-a-Watt device ($30) enables accurate measurement of actual power consumption rather than guessing
Video cards are the second biggest power consumers after CPUs, with mid-range cards drawing 20+ watts even at idle
Power supply efficiency matters significantly—upgrading from 65% to 83% efficiency can provide meaningful savings
Small optimizations like disconnecting unused peripherals and configuring sleep modes add up over time
money_quotes: Have you ever wondered how much it's costing you to leave a computer on 24 hours a day, 7 days a week?
Once you get one, you'll inevitably go through a phase where you run around your home, measuring the power draw of everything you can plug into a wall socket.
So leaving my server on is costing me $500 / year, or $42 per month.
I'm not sure this is widely understood now, but after the CPU, the video card is by far the biggest power consumer in a typical PC.
The worrying number, though, is the idle one. Pay close attention to the video card you use in an 'always-on' machine.
At typical power loads (~90w), the average power supply efficiency is a disappointing 65%.
key_insight: Understanding the true cost of always-on computing requires measuring actual power consumption, and the results—often hundreds of dollars per year per machine—justify investing time in power optimization.
